era_context: The late 17th century: European powers consolidating colonial empires in the Americas, Africa, and Asia. The Ottoman Empire in slow decline; the Qing Dynasty consolidating control over China following the Kangxi Emperor's accession in 1661. The Scientific Revolution advancing in Europe; absolute monarchy at its height in France under Louis XIV. Religious conflict (Catholic-Protestant) still shaping European politics.

events.title: Bacon's Rebellion in Virginia
events.description: Nathaniel Bacon led a violent uprising against Governor William Berkeley's authority, ostensibly over disputes regarding Native American policy and taxation. The rebellion involved both indentured servants and enslaved people, marking a significant moment of cross-racial alliance against colonial authority. The uprising was suppressed by September 1676, but it prompted Virginia's planter elite to shift toward racial slavery as a means of social control, effectively dividing the lower classes along racial lines to prevent future unified resistance.

sources.name: Contemporary accounts by William Berkeley and other colonial officials; later compiled in Virginia colonial records
Historiographic consensus from works by Edmund S. Morgan and others on colonial Virginia

events.certainty_note: Well-documented in colonial records and contemporary correspondence. Dates are secure.

events.title: Treaty of Zurawno (Polish-Ottoman)
events.description: Poland and the Ottoman Empire concluded a peace treaty at Zurawno (modern-day Zvanets, Ukraine) following the Russo-Turkish War and Polish-Ottoman conflicts. The treaty confirmed Ottoman territorial gains in Ukraine and marked a shift in the balance of power in Eastern Europe, with the Ottoman Empire consolidating control over Podolia and Moldavia. This agreement reflected the declining military capacity of Poland relative to Ottoman and Russian powers.

sources.name: Diplomatic records of the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth and Ottoman Porte
Historiographic consensus on Eastern European diplomatic history

events.certainty_note: Treaty date and terms are well-established in diplomatic records.

events.title: King Philip's War concludes in New England
events.description: King Philip's War (Metacom's War), which began in 1675, reached its conclusion in 1676 with the death of Metacom (King Philip) in August and the systematic destruction of Native American resistance in southern New England. The war resulted in the deaths of approximately 600 English colonists and 3,000 Native Americans, and effectively ended indigenous sovereignty in the region. The conflict marked a turning point in colonial-Native American relations, establishing English military dominance and leading to the enslavement and displacement of surviving Native populations.

sources.name: William Hubbard, A Narrative of the Troubles with the Indians in New-England (1677, near-contemporary)

events.certainty_note: Metacom's death is dated to August 1676 in contemporary sources. The war's conclusion is well-documented.

events.title: Kangxi Emperor's Southern Inspection Tour begins
events.description: The Kangxi Emperor undertook the first of his famous Southern Inspection Tours (nanxun), traveling through the Yangtze River valley and southern provinces. These tours served multiple purposes: consolidating Qing control over recently conquered territories, gathering intelligence on local conditions, and projecting imperial authority. The tours became a hallmark of Kangxi's reign and contributed to the stabilization of Qing rule in the south following the suppression of the Three Feudatories Rebellion (1673–1681).

sources.name: Kangxi's own records and imperial edicts; later compiled in Qing imperial archives
Historiographic consensus on Kangxi's reign from modern Qing scholarship

events.certainty_note: The timing and purpose of the Southern Inspection Tours are well-documented in Qing records.

events.title: Battle of the Boyne (preliminary skirmishes in Ireland)
events.description: While the famous Battle of the Boyne occurred in 1690, 1676 saw escalating religious and political tensions in Ireland following the Restoration of Charles II. Catholic-Protestant conflicts intensified, with Protestant fears of Catholic dominance under James II's influence growing. These tensions would culminate in the Williamite War in Ireland (1688–1691), but the groundwork was being laid in the mid-1670s.
key_figures: Charles II (King of England and Ireland)
Catholic and Protestant factions in Ireland

sources.name: Contemporary Irish and English political correspondence and records

events.certainty_note: This entry describes the broader context rather than a specific dated event in 1676. The escalation of tensions is documented but not tied to a single event.

disconfirming_evidence: Bacon's Rebellion is sometimes portrayed as a proto-revolutionary movement against tyranny, but modern scholarship (particularly Edmund S. Morgan) emphasizes that Bacon himself was a planter seeking to expand at Native American expense, not a champion of the lower classes. The rebellion's significance lies not in its egalitarian impulses but in its unintended consequence: the acceleration of racial slavery as a control mechanism. The Treaty of Zurawno is sometimes overshadowed in Western historiography by the later Battle of Vienna (1683), leading to underestimation of Ottoman power in 1676.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1676 is rich in European and colonial North American sources, but heavily skewed toward English-language and Western perspectives. Ottoman, Polish, and Native American sources exist but are less frequently synthesized in English-language historiography. Chinese sources on the Kangxi Emperor are abundant but require specialized knowledge to access. African and Asian events outside the Qing sphere are underrepresented in available sources.

graph_edges.note: Bacon's Rebellion prompted Virginia planters to shift toward racial slavery as a social control mechanism
Conclusion of King Philip's War, which began in 1675
Ottoman consolidation in Eastern Europe set stage for later conflicts with European powers
